coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor based on structure-switching-triggered mimetic enzyme activity for sensitive detection of enrofloxacin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Menglong
Liu, Yiyao
Xiao, Jingyi
Ren, Yi
Gao, Xue
coredata.dc:description: A structure-switching-triggered aptasensor was constructed based on carbon dots (CDs) to detect enrofloxacin (ENR). CDs-functionalized aptamer and two complementary chains (cDNA1 and cDNA2) modified by hemin monomer were self-assembled as sensor. The peroxidase-mimicking activity of hemin monomer can catalyze the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) with yellow fluorescence. Simultaneously, during to the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched. The detection linear range of ENR is 0.1–12 ng mL−1, and the detection limit is 40.7pg mL−1. Furthermore, the aptasensor demonstrated high accuracy and repeatability for the detection of actual aquatic product samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aptasensor
Ratiometric fluorescence
Enrofloxacin

The determination of ENR in real sample
Optimization for the determination of ENR
Detection of ENR in actual sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of CDs (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under excitation at different wavelengths, inset: photographs of CDs solution under visible light (left) and under 365 nm UV light (right). (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs. (D) Infrared absorption spectrum of CDs.

The effect of apt concentration (A) and substrate OPD concentration (B) on fluorescence intensity; (C) The pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of sensing system; (D) Fluorescence intensity effect of detection time.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the aptasensor to different ENR concentrations (a-i: 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.7, 2.5, 4.0, 6.0, 8.0 and 12.0 ng mL−1), inset: corresponding fluorescence image under 365 nm UV lamp; (B) Plots of (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 of versus ENR concentrations.
Specificity assessment of aptasensor for ENR detection.
Schematic diagram of ENR detection based on structure-switching aptasensor.
Determination of ENR in actual samples by standard addition recovery.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor based on structure-switching-triggered mimetic enzyme activity for sensitive detection of enrofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University. National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, Institute of Ocean Research, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, Jinzhou, Liaoning 121013, China

Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products
Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University. National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, Institute of Ocean Research, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, Jinzhou, Liaoning 121013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A structure-switching-triggered aptasensor was constructed based on carbon dots (CDs) to detect enrofloxacin (ENR). CDs-functionalized aptamer and two complementary chains (cDNA1 and cDNA2) modified by hemin monomer were self-assembled as sensor. The peroxidase-mimicking activity of hemin monomer can catalyze the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) with yellow fluorescence. Simultaneously, during to the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched. The detection linear range of ENR is 0.1–12 ng mL−1, and the detection limit is 40.7pg mL−1. Furthermore, the aptasensor demonstrated high accuracy and repeatability for the detection of actual aquatic product samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Enrofloxacin (ENR), also known as ethyl ciprofloxacin as a member of the fluoroquinolones (FQs) family, is a synthetic broad-spectrum antibacterial agent [1]. ENR has been widely used to prevent and treat bacterial diseases in veterinary medicine because of its inexpensive and highly effective [2,3]. Although ENR is still regarded as a valuable tool for the treatment of various diseases in aquaculture and animal husbandry, its own limitations and overuse can lead to the accumulation of antimicrobial residues in animal-derived foods [4]. These residues may pose various health risks, such as irreversible nerve damage, allergic reactions and liver damage [5,6]. Up until now, various approaches such as high-performance liquid chromatography [7], capillary electrophoresis [8] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [9] have been developed for sensitive detection of enrofloxacin. The above methods tend to require expensive instruments and technician. Therefore, it is necessary to develop simple, sensitive and accurate ENR analysis methods.

Fluorescence analysis has been widely applied for the trace detection due to its advantages of short response time, high sensitivity and simple instrumentation [10]. Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been developed as potential substitutes for traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and fluorescent dyes because of their unique optical properties, low cost, and good biocompatibility [11,12]. There were many researches on the detection of tetracycline [13], metal ions [14], chloramphenicol [15] and fluoroquinolones [16] based on CDs fluorescent probe. Although these analytical methods have good sensitivity and convenient operation, they may not have sufficient specificity to identify the target analyte, and the analytical results are susceptible to matrix interference.

Aptamer has been intensively applied in various fields including food safety, environmental monitoring and biomedicine due to their good stability, specificity, and easy modification [17–20]. Aptamer has high affinity for target object but lack of efficient readable signal. Therefore, sensors that respond to fluorescent signals have attracted the attention of researchers [21]. Multiple types of CDs-based aptasensors have been reported for analyte determination [22,23]. However, fluorescence modulation of the fluorophore depends only on the conformational change of the specific aptamer, which limits the sensitivity and application. Structure-switching strategy as a potential aptasensor method can be designed as changes in aptamer and complementary strand structures to trigger and amplify fluorescent signals [24]. Up to now, structure-switching fluorescent aptamers have been employed to detect various analytes with high sensitivity, such as acrylamide [25], chloramphenicol [26] and tobramycin [27]. To our best knowledge, there are few reports on the application of structure-switching ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor to detect ENR.

Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence method based on a structure-switching-triggered aptasensor was developed for the rapid detection of ENR. In the sensing strategy (Scheme 1 ), there are three ssDNA strands, including a CDs-functionalized aptamer (CDs-apt) and two complementary strands. The two complementary single strands are modified with hemin monomers at the 3′ end (denoted as cDNA1) and 5′ end modification (denoted as cDNA2), respectively. In the absence of ENR, CDs-apt, cDNA1 and cDNA2 hybridize with the aptamer and form a structure-switching-triggered sensor, two adjacent hemin molecules on cDNA1 and cDNA2 form a hemin dimer. The small molecule target can form hydrogen bonds with the bases in the secondary structure of the aptamer and use hydrophobic interactions to achieve stable binding and accurate recognition. Furthermore, in the presence of small molecular targets, the binding of these targets to aptamers can induce conformational changes, leading to the bending and folding of the aptamers. This interaction results in alterations to the three-dimensional structure of the aptamers. On this basis, when ENR is present in the sensing system, it can preferentially bind to the aptamer due to its high binding affinity, resulting in the switching of the aptamer sensor. Meanwhile, conversion of heme dimers to monomers with high peroxidase-mimicking activity is triggered. Therefore, after the addition of H2O2, OPD was catalytically oxidized by hemin monomer to generate oxOPD with strong yellow fluorescence. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased due to FRET between CDs and oxOPD. We developed an aptasensor for detection of ENR by ratiometric fluorescence. Furthermore, the structure-switching-triggered aptasensor was further verified for the reliable detection of ENR in practical samples.

ENR aptamer (sequence: 5′-CCC ATC AGG GGG CTA GGC TAA CAC GGT TCG GCT CTC TGA GCC CGG GTT ATT TCA GGG GGA-NH2-3′), cDNA1 (sequence: 5′-CCG GGC TCA GAG AGC CGA ACC-hemin-3′) and cDNA2 (sequence: 5′-hemin-GTG TTA GCC TAG CCC CCT GAT-3′) were synthesized by TAKARA Biotechnology (Dalian, China).

Citric acid (CA) was supplied by Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC), (N-Hydroxy succinimide) (NHS), O-phenylenediamine (OPD), ciprofloxacin (CIP), norfloxacin (NOR), enrofloxacin (ENR), moxifloxacin (MOX), ofloxacin (OFL), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), streptochlorin (Str), nitrofurazone (Nit), chloramphenicol (Chl), furazolidone (Fur), vitamin B2 (Vit), L-Histidine (His), glucose (Glu) and cysteine (Cys) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.

UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on 2550 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were analyzed on a Flouromax-4NIR fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, France). The zeta potential and particle size of materials were analyzed using a zeta potential and laser particle size analyzer 90 Plus Zeta (Brookhaven, USA). Agarose gel electrophoresis was performed on a DYY-6C electrophoresis apparatus (Liuyi, China).

The CDs were synthesized by our previously reported method [28]. The preparation of CDs-apt refered to the carbodiimide method and slightly modified [29]. 170 μL 20 mg mL−1 EDC/NHS and 200 μL CDs solution were mixed and sonicated for 30 min. Then, add 10 μL 50 μM aptamers, and CDs-apt was obtained after shaking at 25 °C for 4 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of structure–switching–triggered aptasensor for ENR detection
ce:section.ce:para: cDNA1 and cDNA2 were added to the CD-apt solution, mixed and incubated for 30 min, and then centrifuged (5000 r/min, 10 min) with an ultrafiltration centrifuge tube (10-kDa) to remove unligated single strands of cDNA.

For the quantitative measurement of ENR, firstly, 40 μL aptasensor was taken and incubated with different concentrations of ENR in Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM) for 30 min. Then, 2 mM OPD and 20 mM H2O2 were added and the mixture was shaken for 30 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence (FL) intensity was measured (λexcitation = 400 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The determination of ENR in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The eel, grass carp and freshwater shrimp samples were purchased from the local market. Three actual samples were deceased when purchased. Samples were peeled to made minced meat, then added 25 mL acetonitrile, homogenized, centrifuged, and extracted the supernatant for use. Whereafter, different concentrations of ENR were added to the real samples and measured by the aptasensor.

Transmission electron microscopy was used to characterize the CDs. It can be seen from the transmission electron microscopy of CDs (Fig. 1 A) that the synthesized CDs have good dispersibility. The CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence, and the absorption spectra was concentrated in the π-π* transitions at about 200–400 nm [30] (Fig. 1C). When the excitation was increased from 300 nm to 420 nm, the CDs fluorescence exhibited a typical excitation-dependent characteristic with a red-shifted emission [31] (Fig. 1B). The infrared spectrum of CDs was analyzed in Fig. 1D, and the stretching vibration of C = O bond was observed at 1600 cm−1-1800 cm−1, which proved the existence of amino groups on the surface of CDs. After the coupling of CDs and aptamer, the nucleic acid electrophoresis speed was slower, due to the increased mass-to-charge ratio of the modified CDs [32] (Fig. S1A at see the Supplementary Materials). The preparation of CDs-apt was verified. Furthermore, the zeta potential of CDs-apt shifted to a positive, which was attributed to the potential neutralization phenomenon caused by the successful modification of aptamers on CDs. (Fig. S1B at see the Supplementary Materials).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the determination of ENR
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the optimal conditions for detecting ENR, the aptamer concentration, OPD concentration, pH value of the system and reaction time were optimized respectively. The optimization results are analyzed by the ratio of (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0, F472 nm and F556 nm are the FL intensity of CDs and oxOPD, respectively, (F556 nm/F472 nm) and (F556 nm/F472 nm)0 are the FL intensity ratios in the presence and absence of ENR, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 enhanced with the increase of aptamer concentration, and (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 decreased when the aptamer concentration was greater than 1.25 μM, which was due to the increase of background fluorescence (F556 nm/F472 nm)0 value caused by overtop aptamer concentration. Similarly, high OPD concentrations of oxidized substrates can also increase blank values. With the increase of OPD concentration, (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 increased first and then decreased. When the concentration of OPD reached 2.5 mM, the ratio of (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 was almost unchanged. This phenomenon occurs because, at this OPD concentration, all the active sites of the nanomaterials have participated in the oxidation reaction, and adding more OPD cannot have a greater effect on the ratio of (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0. The final substrate addition amount was 2.0 mM OPD (Fig. 2B). The ratio of (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 was maximum at pH 7 (Fig. 2C). Because the hydrogen bond of base complementation was unstable under acidic or basic conditions, the triple-stranded structure of the aptasensor was broken. In addition, the reaction time after the addition of ENR was investigated, (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 reaching a maximum and tending to stabilize after 25 min of incubation (Fig. 2D). It shows that when the oxidation duration is 25 min, the substrate has been completely reacted, thereby achieving optimal oxidation efficacy. Therefore, the optimal reaction time for the sensing system is 25 min.

The sensitivity for ENR detection was studied. With the increasing concentrations of ENR (0 −12ng mL−1), the emission of oxOPD at 556 nm was enhanced and the emission of CDs at 472 nm decreased (Fig. 3 A). A good linearity was observed between (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 and ENR concentrations of 0.1-12ng mL−1 (Fig. 3B). The regression equation was (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 = 1.1064 + 0.2663CENR (ng mL−1), (R2 = 0.9976). The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3δ/S (δ is the standard deviation of 11 blank measurements, S is the slope of the standard curve) is 40.7 pg mL−1. We compared with various methods previously reported for ENR detection work, which was comparable to or better than existing methods in terms of linear range and LOD (Table S1, see the Supplementary Materials). The high sensitivity of the aptasensor was mainly attributed to the signal amplification of the hydrogen peroxide mimetic enzyme and the self-calibration of the ratiometric fluorescence signal.

To verify that the sensing system can effectively detect ENR, we conducted a feasibility evaluation. As shown in Fig. S2, since OPD is susceptible to oxidation, when exposed to air, a small amount of OPD will also be oxidized to oxOPD. However, in the detection of ENR, the impact of these autoxidation processes is minimal compared to the oxidation of OPD by hemin monomer with high peroxidase-like activity. Therefore, these processes will not significantly affect the accuracy of the detection results. The fluorescence intensity of oxOPD was very low without ENR (curve a) due to the low catalytic activity of the aptasensor (Fig. S3A at see the Supplementary Materials). In the presence of ENR (curve b), the aptamer preferentially binded to ENR to break the triple-stranded structure of the aptasensor, triggering the conversion of hemin dimer to monomer with high catalytic activity. In the absence of ENR, there were a characteristic absorption peak at 378 nm for the hemin dimer (curve a) and a characteristic peak at 257 nm for DNA [33,34] (Fig. S3B at see the Supplementary Materials). However, when ENR was present, the hemin dimer was dissociated into hemin monomer, and the absorption peak was red-shifted to around 400 nm (curve b). The results showed that the presence of ENR caused the hemin on the sensor to switch from a dimer to two monomers. The oxOPD fluorescence signal was triggered by the highly catalytic oxidative activity of hemin monomer. As a ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor to detect ENR, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased with the increase of ENR concentration (Fig. 3A). The fluorescence intensity was measured after incubation of CDs-apt with ENR, the fluorescence intensity at 472 nm was unaffected (Fig. S4 at see the Supplementary Materials). The quenching of CDs in the aptasensor may be due to fluorescence energy transfer that overlaped with the oxOPD spectrum. Furthermore, our previous work had demonstrated that FRET occured between CD and oxOPD (Fig. S5 at see the Supplementary Materials). Therefore, the detection of ENR was accomplished by the ratiometric fluorescence response signal induced by the structural transition of hemin after specific recognition.

The specificity experiments were evaluated via detecting various substances including TC, Chl, Str, Nit, Fur, CIP, NOR, MOX, GAT and OFL. As shown in Fig. 4 A, all the other substances at the concentration of 0.8 μg mL−1 could not significantly affect the (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 value. However, 8 ng mL−1 of ENR had a significant enhancing effect on the (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 value. In addition, the 2.2 mM concentration of potentially interfering ions and other substances (Vit, His, Glu and Cys, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Ca2+) had no significant effect on the (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0 value (Fig. 4B). Since Cu2+ can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to yield the final products H2O and O2, the presence of O2 in these products may also oxidize OPD to oxOPD, potentially interfering with experimental results. Therefore, we incorporated the maximum allowable concentration of Cu2+ as specified in the document (GB 5749–2022) for testing purposes. Ultimately, we concluded that, compared to ENR, the presence of Cu2+ exerts a smaller influence on the ratio of the sensing system (F556 nm/F472 nm)/(F556 nm/F472 nm)0. Therefore, the aptasensor had specific recognition performance for ENR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ENR in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: The treated aquatic product samples were determined by the aptasensor, and no ENR was detected. Then the standard addition recoveries of eel, grass carp and freshwater shrimp were 97.2 %–101.5 %, 97.5 %–107.1 % and 99.8 %–102.1 %, respectively (Table 1 ). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 4.4 %. In addition, commercial colloidal gold immunochromatographic detection card (GICD, ISO9001: 2015) was used to verify the accuracy of the aptasensor. As presented in Fig. S6, the control line (C) on the colloidal gold rapid detection card exhibited a purple-red color, indicating that the results obtained from the detection card were valid. As the concentration of ENR increased, the intensity of the purple-red color on the detection line (T) gradually diminished. The detection results obtained by the sensor and GICD were consistent. The aptasensor had accuracy and repeatability for ENR detection in actual aquatic products.

Aptamers are employed as the recognition element of the aptasensor, CDs and oxOPD as the fluorescent response signal of the aptasensor, a structure-switching ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor was successfully developed for ENR detection. Based on the structural change of hemin caused by the structural change of aptasensor, hemin monomer mimetic enzyme triggers fluorescence response, which has the effect of detection signal amplification. The specificity experiment and the recovery determination of actual aquatic products verify that the method has the characteristics of stability and accuracy. Therefore, the aptasensor satisfies the needs of ENR residue detection in aquatic products.

Menglong Liu: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yiyao Liu: Validation, Data curation. Jingyi Xiao: Validation, Data curation. Yi Ren: Validation, Data curation. Xue Gao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2024.141684.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
